conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have two children - Alex, who's 12, and Blake, who's 6.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great that you have two children, Alex (12) and Blake (6).

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you a few more questions.

Are you currently responsible for Alex and Blake? By this I mean, do they live with you, and are you the person who is mainly responsible for their day-to-day care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Well, it's a bit complicated. Alex doesn't live with me - he lives with his ex-partner. I do help out a bit with costs for him though.

Blake lives with me, but I'm actually fostering him and the local authority provides maintenance for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for explaining your situation. This is helpful information.

Let me ask a few follow-up questions to get a complete picture:

For Blake, who you're fostering - are you fostering him through a formal arrangement with the local authority, or is this an informal private arrangement?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's a formal fostering arrangement through the local authority. They give me maintenance payments for Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

Now, regarding Alex - you mentioned he lives with his ex-partner. Can I ask:

1. Does your ex-partner receive Child Benefit for Alex?
2. And do you contribute towards Alex's costs by paying more than the Child Benefit amount each week to help with his care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, my ex-partner already claims Child Benefit for Alex. As for what I contribute - I help out with things like clothes and bits and pieces, but it's only about £20 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me just confirm a couple more details to make sure I have the complete picture:

1. Are both Alex and Blake under 20 years old and not in full-time paid work or receiving certain benefits themselves?
2. Are you and the children living in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex is 12 and Blake is 6, so they're both well under 20. Neither of them work or receive any benefits themselves. And yes, we all live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary

Unfortunately, you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake. Here's why:

**For Alex (age 12):**
- Alex doesn't live with you - he lives with your ex-partner who is already claiming Child Benefit for him
- Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time
- To be eligible, you need to be the person responsible for the child, meaning they live with you and you're mainly responsible for their day-to-day care
- While you contribute around £20 per week, this is less than the Child Benefit amount and wouldn't be sufficient grounds to transfer the claim

**For Blake (age 6):**
- Blake is in a formal fostering arrangement with the local authority who provides maintenance payments
- Under UK Child Benefit rules, you cannot claim Child Benefit for a child who is being "looked after" by the local authority
- Even though Blake lives with you and you provide day-to-day care, children in formal local authority foster care arrangements are excluded from Child Benefit eligibility

I'm sorry this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. Is there anything else I can help clarify about this decision?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 12 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 6 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority; Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child is fostered and the council pays for their accommodation or maintenance
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 12 years old and lives with your ex-partner, who is already claiming Child Benefit for him. To be eligible for Child Benefit, you must be the person responsible for the child, meaning the child lives with you and you are mainly responsible for their day-to-day care. Since Alex doesn't live with you and your ex-partner is already claiming Child Benefit for him, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Alex. Additionally, only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any given time. While you do contribute around £20 per week towards Alex's costs, this is less than the Child Benefit weekly amount, which would not be sufficient grounds to transfer the claim even if the circumstances were different.

Blake is 6 years old and lives with you under a formal fostering arrangement with the local authority, who provides maintenance payments. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you cannot claim Child Benefit for a child who is being looked after by the local authority (a 'looked after child'). When a child is formally fostered through a local authority arrangement with maintenance provided by the authority, they are considered a 'looked after child' and Child Benefit is not payable. This rule applies even though Blake lives with you and you provide day-to-day care.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (12) who lives with an ex-partner who already claims Child Benefit for him, and Blake (6) who is formally fostered through the local authority. Unfortunately, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for either child. For Alex, only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and the ex-partner is already claiming it. For Blake, children in formal local authority foster care arrangements are excluded from Child Benefit eligibility as they are 'looked after children' receiving local authority maintenance.

This rule applies even though Blake lives with you and you provide day-to-day care.

For Blake, children in formal local authority foster care arrangements are excluded from Child Benefit eligibility as they are 'looked after children' receiving local authority maintenance.
